questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer medications to a client. To ensure client safety, which action should the nurse perform FIRST?

choices.text: Check the client's identification using two identifiers.
Explain the purpose of the medication to the client.
Perform the third medication check at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Ensuring the 'Right Patient' is a crucial first step in the medication administration process according to the 'Rights of Medication Administration'. This is accomplished by using at least two client identifiers (e.g., name and date of birth, name and medical record number) and comparing them against the medication administration record (MAR). While explaining the medication (C) and performing checks (D) are vital, verifying the patient's identity (B) must occur before proceeding. Documentation (A) occurs after administration.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: A client is prescribed warfarin (Coumadin), an oral anticoagulant. Which laboratory test result requires monitoring specifically for this medication's therapeutic effect?
questions.rationale: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Its therapeutic effect is monitored using the Prothrombin Time (PT) and, more commonly, the International Normalized Ratio (INR) (D). The aPTT (A) is used to monitor heparin therapy. H&H (B) assesses for anemia or blood loss but not warfarin's anticoagulant effect directly. Platelet count (C) assesses for thrombocytopenia, not warfarin efficacy.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client diagnosed with heart failure. Which assessment findings would indicate worsening of the condition? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Weight loss of 1 kg in 24 hours
Presence of crackles in the lung bases
Pitting edema rated at 3+ in lower extremities
Client reports increased shortness of breath when lying flat

questions.rationale: Worsening heart failure typically involves increased fluid volume overload. Crackles (B) indicate pulmonary edema (left-sided failure). Increased pitting edema (C) and JVD (F) indicate systemic venous congestion (right-sided failure). Orthopnea (shortness of breath when lying flat) (D) indicates increased pulmonary congestion. Weight gain (not loss, A) and increased abdominal girth (due to ascites, not E) would also signify worsening fluid retention.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: According to Erikson's stages of psychosocial development, which developmental task is characteristic of the toddler age group (1-3 years)?
questions.rationale: Erikson's stage for toddlers (1-3 years) is Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (D). During this stage, toddlers focus on developing a sense of personal control over physical skills and a sense of independence. Trust vs. Mistrust (A) is infancy (birth-1 year). Initiative vs. Guilt (B) is preschool (3-6 years). Industry vs. Inferiority (C) is school-age (6-12 years).

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: A laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. The nurse notes the amniotic fluid is greenish-brown. What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Administer oxygen to the mother via non-rebreather mask.
Perform a vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation.
Assess the fetal heart rate (FHR) and pattern.

questions.rationale: Greenish-brown amniotic fluid indicates the presence of meconium, which can be a sign of fetal distress (hypoxia) either past or present. Meconium aspiration syndrome is a potential complication for the newborn. The immediate priority is to assess fetal well-being by evaluating the FHR and pattern (C) for signs of distress (e.g., bradycardia, late decelerations, decreased variability). While notifying the provider (D) and potentially administering oxygen (A) are important, assessing the FHR takes precedence. A vaginal examination (B) is contraindicated when membranes are ruptured and meconium is present due to increased infection risk and potential to advance cord/meconium.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: A client diagnosed with major depressive disorder states, "Nothing matters anymore. It's hopeless." Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?

choices.text: "You shouldn't feel that way; things will get better."
"Why do you think everything is hopeless?"
"It sounds like you're feeling very discouraged right now."
"Let's focus on something positive, like your hobbies."

questions.rationale: Reflecting the client's feelings ('feeling very discouraged') validates their experience and opens the door for further exploration (C). Offering false reassurance (A) dismisses the client's feelings. Asking 'why' questions (B) can sound accusatory and put the client on the defensive. Changing the subject abruptly (D) avoids the client's expressed concern.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD). Which finding would indicate a potential need for oxygen therapy adjustment or evaluation?

choices.text: Oxygen saturation (SpO2) of 91% on room air at rest

questions.rationale: In some COPD clients, particularly those with chronic hypercapnia (CO2 retention), excessive oxygen administration can depress the hypoxic drive to breathe, leading to increased CO2 retention, respiratory acidosis, and CNS effects like confusion or drowsiness (carbon dioxide narcosis) (C). While SpO2 of 91% (B) might be acceptable or baseline for some COPD clients, a change in mental status is a more urgent sign requiring assessment. A respiratory rate of 18 (A) and minimal sputum production (D) are not concerning findings in this context.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: A nurse is teaching a client about taking gentamicin, an aminoglycoside antibiotic. Which potential adverse effects should the nurse emphasize? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Aminoglycosides like gentamicin carry significant risks of nephrotoxicity (damage to kidneys) (A) and ototoxicity (damage to the inner ear affecting hearing and balance) (D). These require close monitoring of renal function (BUN, creatinine) and assessing for hearing loss, tinnitus, or vertigo. While GI upset (E) can occur with many antibiotics, kidney and ear toxicity are the major concerns specific to aminoglycosides. Hepatotoxicity (B), photosensitivity (C), and constipation (F) are not typical adverse effects of aminoglycosides.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: A nurse witnesses another healthcare provider failing to follow proper hand hygiene protocols before caring for a client. What is the nurse's primary ethical responsibility in this situation?

choices.text: Report the incident directly to the hospital administrator.
Document the observation in the client's medical record.
Address the issue directly with the healthcare provider involved.
Ignore the observation as it was a single occurrence.

questions.rationale: The primary ethical responsibility is patient safety. The most direct and often most effective initial step is to address the concern directly and professionally with the colleague (C). This provides an opportunity for immediate correction and education. If the behavior persists or is egregious, escalating the concern through the chain of command (e.g., charge nurse, manager) is necessary. Reporting directly to administration (A) without attempting collegial correction is not the first step. Documenting in the patient record (B) is not appropriate for staff issues. Ignoring a breach in infection control practice (D) puts patients at risk and fails to uphold professional standards.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: A common childhood illness characterized by a high fever for several days followed by the appearance of a maculopapular rash after the fever breaks is known as Roseola infantum, caused by Human __________ 6 or 7.
questions.rationale: Roseola infantum (also called sixth disease or exanthem subitum) is typically caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or less commonly HHV-7. The classic presentation involves 3-5 days of high fever which resolves abruptly, followed by the onset of a non-itchy, pinkish-red maculopapular rash that often starts on the trunk and spreads.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a postpartum client 24 hours after a vaginal delivery. The nurse palpates the uterine fundus. Where should the nurse expect to locate the fundus?

choices.text: At the level of the symphysis pubis
Midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis

questions.rationale: After delivery, the uterus begins the process of involution. By 24 hours postpartum, the fundus should typically be firm and located approximately 1 cm (or one fingerbreadth) below the umbilicus (B). It continues to descend about 1 cm per day, becoming non-palpable abdominally by around day 10-14. Locating it at the symphysis (A) occurs much later. Midway (C) would be around day 6. Above the umbilicus (D) immediately post-delivery is possible, but it should be below the umbilicus by 24 hours.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: A client taking haloperidol (Haldol) develops uncontrollable restlessness, pacing, and fidgeting. The nurse recognizes these symptoms as indicative of which extrapyramidal side effect (EPS)?
questions.rationale: Akathisia (B) is characterized by motor restlessness, with an inability to sit still, constant pacing, fidgeting, and agitation. This differs from tardive dyskinesia (A), which involves involuntary, repetitive movements, particularly of the face and mouth. Acute dystonia (C) presents as sudden muscle spasms and abnormal posturing. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) involves tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and shuffling gait.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: A pregnant patient's last menstrual period (LMP) began on June 10th. Using Nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (EDD)?
questions.rationale: Nägele's rule is calculated by subtracting 3 months from the first day of the LMP and adding 7 days. LMP: June 10th. Subtract 3 months: March 10th. Add 7 days: March 17th. Therefore, the EDD is March 17th.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a patient in the third trimester of pregnancy. Which physiological change is considered normal during this period?

choices.text: Increase in respiratory rate and depth.
Decrease in heart rate by 10 bpm.

questions.rationale: During pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, progesterone causes increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to CO2 and maternal oxygen demands increase, leading to increased respiratory rate and tidal volume (depth) (B). Total blood volume significantly increases (A). Heart rate typically increases by 10-15 bpm (C). Dependent edema often worsens in the third trimester due to pressure from the enlarging uterus on pelvic veins and increased fluid volume (D).

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: A nurse is educating a pregnant client about danger signs that require immediate reporting to the healthcare provider. Which signs should be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Mild ankle edema at the end of the day
Sudden gush of fluid from the vagina
Occasional nausea in the first trimester
Absence of fetal movement for >12 hours

questions.rationale: Vaginal bleeding (A) can indicate issues like placenta previa or abruption. Sudden gush of fluid (C) suggests rupture of membranes. Severe headache (D) can be a sign of preeclampsia. Decreased or absent fetal movement (F) can indicate fetal distress. Mild ankle edema (B) is common and usually benign. Occasional nausea (E) is typical morning sickness.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: A patient in active labor has ruptured membranes. The nurse notes a baseline fetal heart rate (FHR) of 135 bpm. During a contraction, the FHR drops abruptly to 90 bpm and returns quickly to baseline after the contraction ends. What is the most likely cause of this FHR pattern?
questions.rationale: The FHR pattern described is characteristic of variable decelerations, which are caused by umbilical cord compression (B). Key features include: abrupt onset, rapid drop (often below 100 bpm), variable timing in relation to contractions, and rapid return to baseline. Variable decelerations have a distinctive 'V' or 'U' shape on the monitor. Uteroplacental insufficiency (A) causes late decelerations, which have a gradual onset and recovery, occur after the peak of the contraction, and have a uniform shape. Maternal hypotension (C) typically causes prolonged decelerations. Fetal head compression (D) causes early decelerations, which mirror the contraction and rarely drop below 100 bpm.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Musculoskeletal)
questions.text: A client has just undergone a total hip arthroplasty (THA). Which instruction is crucial for the nurse to include in the client's postoperative teaching to prevent hip dislocation?

choices.text: Avoid extending the hip beyond neutral position.
Cross the legs only when sitting in a chair.
Use an abduction pillow between the legs, especially when turning.
Elevate the affected leg when sitting.

questions.rationale: Maintaining hip abduction (keeping the legs apart) is critical after THA to prevent dislocation of the new joint, especially with a posterior approach. An abduction pillow (C) helps maintain this position, particularly during turning or while in bed. Hip flexion beyond 90 degrees, adduction (crossing legs - B), and internal rotation should be avoided. Extending the hip (A) is generally not restricted. Elevating the leg (D) helps with edema but doesn't directly prevent dislocation.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: A client is receiving intravenous heparin therapy for a deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Which laboratory value should the nurse monitor closely to assess the therapeutic effect of heparin?
questions.rationale: Heparin's anticoagulant effect is primarily monitored using the activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) (C). The therapeutic range for aPTT during heparin therapy is typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the baseline control value. PT/INR (A, B) are used to monitor warfarin therapy. Platelet count (D) is monitored to detect heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), a potential adverse effect, but not to assess therapeutic anticoagulation.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is inserting an indwelling urinary catheter into a female client. After inserting the catheter, no urine return is observed. What is the most likely reason for this?

choices.text: The catheter is obstructed by sediment.
The catheter has been inserted into the vagina.

questions.rationale: In female clients, accidentally inserting the urinary catheter into the vagina instead of the urethra is a common reason for lack of urine return (C). If this occurs, the nurse should leave the misplaced catheter in the vagina as a landmark, obtain a new sterile catheter kit, and attempt insertion again, aiming slightly more anteriorly and superiorly. While dehydration (A) or obstruction (B) could cause low output, complete lack of initial return often points to incorrect placement. Premature balloon inflation (D) can cause urethral trauma but would usually allow some urine return.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: A 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. Which clinical sign would the nurse assess for that is characteristic of meningeal irritation?
questions.rationale: Meningeal irritation causes inflammation of the meninges, leading to specific signs. Brudzinski's sign is positive when passive flexion of the child's neck causes flexion of the hips and knees (C). Kernig's sign is positive when resistance or pain occurs upon extending the knee while the hip is flexed. Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is also common (B is incorrect). A negative Kernig's sign (A) indicates absence of that specific sign of irritation. A sunken fontanelle (D) suggests dehydration rather than meningitis, and by age 4, the fontanelles have typically closed.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: The thinning and shortening of the cervix during the first stage of labor is referred to as ____________.
questions.rationale: Effacement describes the process where the cervix, normally about 2-3 cm long, progressively thins out and shortens, drawing up into the lower uterine segment. It is measured in percentages (0% to 100%). Dilation refers to the opening of the cervical os.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: A client with anorexia nervosa has a nursing diagnosis of Imbalanced Nutrition: Less Than Body Requirements. Which intervention is most important for the nurse to implement initially?

choices.text: Explore the client's feelings about weight gain.
Establish a structured meal plan with specific times and amounts.
Provide education on the long-term health consequences of malnutrition.
Encourage the client to lead group therapy sessions.

questions.rationale: For clients with anorexia nervosa, establishing nutritional stability is a priority. Implementing a structured meal plan with clear expectations for intake (B) provides routine and minimizes negotiation or avoidance behaviors around food. While exploring feelings (A) and providing education (C) are important therapeutic interventions, addressing the immediate nutritional deficit through structured eating is often the initial focus. Leading group therapy (D) is not appropriate initially and would not address the nutritional deficit.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Immune)
questions.text: A client receiving a blood transfusion develops flushing, itching, and hives within the first 15 minutes. What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Flushing, itching (pruritus), and hives (urticaria) are signs of a potential allergic reaction, which can range from mild to severe (anaphylaxis). The absolute first priority when any transfusion reaction is suspected is to stop the transfusion immediately (C) to prevent further administration of the offending agent. Subsequent actions include assessing vital signs (D), maintaining IV access with normal saline, notifying the provider and blood bank, and administering medications like antihistamines (A) as ordered. Slowing the rate (B) is never appropriate when a reaction is suspected.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: A client is prescribed digoxin (Lanoxin) for heart failure. The nurse should instruct the client to monitor for and report which signs and symptoms of potential digoxin toxicity? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Visual disturbances (e.g., yellow halos, blurred vision)

questions.rationale: Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic range, and toxicity is a significant risk. Common signs include gastrointestinal disturbances (nausea, vomiting, anorexia) (C), cardiac rhythm changes (especially bradycardia (E) or heart block), neurological symptoms (confusion, lethargy) (F), and characteristic visual disturbances (blurred vision, yellow/green halos around lights) (A). Tachycardia (B) is less common than bradycardia. Muscle cramps (D) are often associated with electrolyte imbalances (like hypokalemia, which potentiates digoxin toxicity) but are not a direct sign of digoxin toxicity itself.

questions.title: Leadership & Management
questions.text: A nurse manager observes that a staff nurse consistently fails to complete documentation by the end of the shift, requiring other nurses to finish it. What is the manager's most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Assign a mentor to the staff nurse.
Discuss the observation directly with the staff nurse to identify barriers and expectations.
Report the nurse's behavior to the board of nursing.

questions.rationale: Effective management involves addressing performance issues directly and seeking to understand the cause. The initial step should be a private conversation with the staff nurse to discuss the specific observations, clarify expectations regarding timely documentation, and explore potential reasons or barriers (e.g., time management issues, workload, lack of knowledge) (C). Disciplinary action (A) or reporting to the board (D) would be premature without investigation and attempts at corrective action. Assigning a mentor (B) might be part of a solution but only after understanding the root cause.

questions.title: Community Health Nursing
questions.text: Which level of prevention focuses on early detection and prompt treatment of disease to prevent complications?
questions.rationale: Secondary prevention (B) aims to identify diseases in their earliest stages and intervene promptly to slow or stop their progression. Examples include screening tests (e.g., mammograms, blood pressure checks) and treating diseases early. Primary prevention (A) focuses on preventing disease occurrence in the first place (e.g., vaccinations, health education). Tertiary prevention (C) focuses on rehabilitation and reducing complications in people already diagnosed with a disease. Primordial prevention (D) involves addressing broad determinants of health to prevent risk factors from developing.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Respiratory)
questions.text: A client with pneumonia has a nursing diagnosis of Ineffective Airway Clearance. Which nursing intervention is most appropriate to help liquefy respiratory secretions?

choices.text: Positioning the client flat in bed.

questions.rationale: Adequate hydration helps to thin and liquefy respiratory secretions, making them easier to expectorate (cough up). Encouraging fluid intake (unless contraindicated) is a key intervention for ineffective airway clearance (B). Cough suppressants (A) should generally be avoided unless the cough is nonproductive and exhausting, as coughing helps clear secretions. Positioning flat (C) can hinder lung expansion; Fowler's or Semi-Fowler's is preferred. Limiting activity (D) may be necessary based on oxygenation, but mobilization actually helps mobilize secretions.
